# Questions in Livestream 4

Live Stream #4: The More Bugs Stomped, The More Appear.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=146" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26</a> | Was the three-tiered cosmology of Egypt and other ancient cultures literal, or more metaphorical? | He thinks these cosmologies used analogical language and symbols rather than exact literal scientific models. | He distinguishes ancient symbolic world descriptions from modern scientific diagrams. In his view, the underworld, earth, and heavens language works analogically and religiously rather than as a literal engineering map of the universe. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=325" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:25</a> | Does the biblical conquest account align with 13th-12th century Canaan outside Jericho and Hazor? | Yes, if the text is read as occupation and displacement rather than fire destruction of every city. | He argues that the Bible only explicitly requires burning a few cities, especially Jericho, Hazor, and Ai. For other places, occupation, displacement, and taking over houses and farms better match the archaeological pattern than universal destruction layers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:00</a> | What happened to Pharaoh's other sons after the heir took the throne? | Usually they were not killed; they moved into other roles such as the army, scribal work, or other professions. | He says Egypt normally had an identified crown prince, so rival sons did not have to be eliminated as a rule. Unless a succession crisis arose, other royal sons could enter military, scribal, priestly, or administrative careers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=779" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:59</a> | When will the Ark book come out in Logos? | He was not sure and said publishers do not always keep authors informed about electronic editions. | He says he does not know the Logos schedule and notes that authors are often not closely informed about every electronic edition. Publication format decisions belong largely to the publisher after the manuscript is complete. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=870" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:30</a> | What is the current status of Ebla studies? | Ebla texts are being published, but much of the material is ordinary administrative material rather than headline-grabbing biblical parallels. | He says Ebla studies recovered from earlier sensational claims and now proceeds through slower translation and publication. Much of the corpus is administrative, such as contracts, receipts, and letters, so its importance is often historical and synchronistic rather than spectacular. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=987" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:27</a> | If newly discovered Lachish ostraca are Hebrew and Late Bronze Age IB, around 1400 BC, would that support an early exodus date? | He is skeptical, especially of Petrovich's early Hebrew claims, and does not treat it as good support for an early exodus. | He treats the argument as dependent on Petrovich's early-Hebrew proposal, which he finds unpersuasive. Because he sees that proposal as already weak, he does not accept the Lachish claim as significant evidence for an early exodus date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=1058" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:38</a> | Could Israel have formed from descendants of Egyptian soldiers in Canaan who married local women? | He notes Egyptian garrison towns persisted and show some local mixing, but he does not present that as the origin of Israel. | He acknowledges that Egyptian garrisons in Canaan continued for a time and could show local mixing in pottery and marriage patterns. But he presents those communities as shrinking or isolated, not as the main source for Israel's emergence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=1228" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:28</a> | What do you think about Judah and Levi being absent from the Song of Deborah? | He rejects arguments from silence; Judges presents parallel tribal histories, and not every tribe appears in every episode. | He says absence from one poem cannot prove a tribe did not exist. Judges presents overlapping tribal stories with selective aims, so not every tribe is expected to appear in every conflict. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=1408" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:28</a> | How did New Kingdom Egypt control Nubia, and was it like control over the Levant? | Nubia was controlled more tightly, with a viceroy and garrisons, because Egypt depended heavily on Nubian gold. | He contrasts Nubia with Canaan: Nubia had a viceroy, garrisons, and tighter administration, while Canaan was often managed through tribute and vassals. Nubia mattered intensely because of gold and other resources, so Egypt invested more direct control there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=1603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:43</a> | Where did the Hyksos go after being expelled from Egypt, and do they appear in the Bible? | He says they are not mentioned in the Bible; their leaders fled toward Gaza and were destroyed, while Avaris fell and captives were taken. | He explains that Hyksos refers to foreign rulers over a Semitic population at Avaris, not a biblical ethnic label. After Ahmose's campaign, the leaders fled toward Gaza and were destroyed, while the city fell and captives were taken. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=1761" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:21</a> | How is the Merneptah Stele proof for a late exodus date if it is often used for an early date? | He says it fits a late date because Israel appears as an unsettled people group while Hurrians still remain in Canaan. | He says the stele matters because Israel is named as a people rather than a settled city or land. Combined with other Canaanite groups still present, that fits his picture of Israel early in the conquest period after a late exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=1983" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:03</a> | When Solon heard the Atlantis story, were the Egyptians philosophers in the Greek sense or educated in Egyptian lore and religion? | He frames them as Egyptian priests or learned figures whose religious discourse was later transformed into Greek philosophical discourse. | He describes Greek visitors engaging Egyptian priests and learned religious specialists. What began as Egyptian religious or mythic discourse could then be translated into Greek philosophical categories. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=2230" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:10</a> | Is Ramesses III's account of defeating and resettling the Philistines correct? | He thinks Ramesses III probably resettled some, but Egyptian control likely did not last long after he went home. | He accepts that Ramesses III campaigned and may have resettled some Philistines. But because Egypt's Levantine control weakened soon afterward, he suspects the Philistines reoccupied or retained coastal cities after Egyptian withdrawal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=2495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:35</a> | What is the case for a Hyksos ruler, rather than an Egyptian ruler, during Joseph's time? | He points to chariots, Goshen/Avaris, and cultural similarity between Jacob's family and Amorite Semites at Avaris. | He lists several converging clues: Joseph's chariot fits the Hyksos-linked chariot context, Goshen points near Avaris, and Jacob's family would have shared language and customs with Amorite Semites there. A Hyksos court therefore makes Joseph's rise more culturally plausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=2872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:52</a> | Was Moses known by another Egyptian name, and who was his royal stepmother? | He thinks Moses likely had a theophoric Egyptian name and suggests his adoptive mother may have been tied to the family of Ramesses I or Seti I before royal elevation. | He follows the idea that Moses may preserve only part of a longer Egyptian theophoric name. For the adoptive mother, he suggests a woman in the family that later became royal, so she could be remembered as Pharaoh's daughter after that family's elevation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=3154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:34</a> | Can the theology of the Pentateuch help date it earlier? | Yes, he says patriarchal religious customs and Late Bronze Age linguistic features support early source material. | He points to practices such as standing stones and to language features as evidence for old source material. He allows that some might call these memories, but he thinks the cumulative religious and linguistic setting supports substantial antiquity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=3302" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:02</a> | Does comparing 12th-century Hittites with 8th-9th century Hittites undermine Egyptian chronology? | No. He says the Bible distinguishes later "kings of the Hittites" from the earlier imperial Hittite context. | He rejects the comparison because biblical monarchy-period references to Hittites fit Neo-Hittite polities, not the old Hittite empire. In his view, using this to attack Egyptian chronology confuses distinct historical settings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=3419" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:59</a> | What is the origin story of the Egyptians, and why did they become influential? | The Nile is central: it supported settlement, agriculture, trade, and eventually a unified powerful kingdom. | He says Egypt's rise begins with the Nile's capacity to support agriculture, travel, settlement, and trade. Those advantages eventually allowed Upper and Lower Egypt to unify and become a major power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=3546" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:06</a> | What do you think about Associates for Biblical Research's Shiloh excavations? | He says they may find interesting material but is highly cautious about their early-exodus-driven interpretations. | He allows that the excavation may recover interesting objects and that the fieldwork can have value. His caution is about interpretation, because he thinks ABR's conclusions are strongly shaped by an early-exodus agenda. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=3739" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:19</a> | If Merneptah faced Israel during the conquest, why is no Egyptian incursion mentioned in Joshua? | Joshua has pedagogical aims and no explicit mention; he says possible consequences need further study. | He says Joshua is not written to record every regional military event. Merneptah's action could have been a campaign rather than a conquest, and the absence of a direct mention leaves room for study rather than disproving the connection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=3881" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:41</a> | How important is Khirbet Qeiyafa for the debate over the united monarchy? | He does not think it is relevant and criticizes the site identification and publication practices. | He does not see the site as decisive for the united monarchy debate because he questions its identification and relevance. He also stresses the need for detailed publication, since excavated sites cannot simply be dug again. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=4051" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:31</a> | What is your favorite bad archaeology trope? | The Indiana Jones-style fedora, because a wool hat is impractical in desert fieldwork. | He picks the fedora because it is visually iconic but impractical for desert archaeology. In real field conditions, broad cotton hats make more sense than a hot wool hat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=4138" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:58</a> | Do you wish Petrie and other early archaeologists had left certain sites alone? | Not necessarily, since some sites were later destroyed, but he wishes some excavations had been done more carefully. | He gives a mixed answer: some early excavation preserved information from sites later damaged or lost, but methods were often rushed by modern standards. He credits Petrie for improving archaeology while still wishing for better recording in some cases. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=4274" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:14</a> | What is your favorite era of Egyptian history, and what do you think of Robert Schoch's Sphinx dating? | His favorite is the New Kingdom; he rejects very early Sphinx dating and places it in the Old Kingdom. | He favors the New Kingdom because of its artifacts, furniture, and geopolitical importance. On the Sphinx, he says its Old Kingdom architectural context makes very early dating untenable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=4379" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:59</a> | How developed was ancient Egypt's astronomical knowledge? | It existed and was tied to medicine and astrology, but it was not precise astronomy in a modern sense. | He says Egypt had constellations, Sothic-cycle observations, and astral associations in medicine and astrology. But he does not treat it as modern-style mathematical astronomy with precise planetary calculations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=4534" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:34</a> | How much influence did the Hittites have on Israelite theology and history? | He thinks Hittite influence on Israelite religion was probably minimal by the time Israel was establishing itself in Canaan. | He notes that Hittite religion had astral and other features, but doubts it strongly shaped Israelite religion. By Israel's settlement period, Egyptian control and later Neo-Hittite survivals matter more than the old Hittite empire. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=4752" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:12</a> | Did the Israelites think they were worshiping Yahweh in the golden calf incident? | No. He reads it as default bovine deity worship, not aberrant Yahweh worship. | He distinguishes the golden calf from later high-place Yahweh worship. In his reading, Moses' absence led Israel to revert to familiar bovine deity worship rather than to make an acceptable image of Yahweh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=4909" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:49</a> | Is the Egyptian *ms* etymology for Moses linguistically possible? | He is not persuaded and prefers Schneider's *m-she* explanation. | He says the traditional *ms* derivation does not fit the consonants of Moshe well enough. He prefers Schneider's proposal connecting the name to an Egyptian m-she element, while leaving details cautious. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HbJo9pcyNgE?t=4986" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:06</a> | Could the golden calf have been a ceramic calf covered with gold leaf? | He thinks not; central cult images in that setting were more likely made of metal rather than ceramic with gold leaf. | He says central cult images in that world were usually made of precious or symbolically appropriate metals. Ceramic personal figurines existed, and gold over wood is possible, but a ceramic core with gold leaf seems unlikely for the golden calf. |
